german_unity_day.intro: October 3rd is the national day of the Federal Republic of Germany and commemorates the reunification of West and East Germany in 1990.
german_unity_day.history: When the Berlin Wall fell on November 9th, 1989, German division became politically surmountable. The ‘Unification Treaty' set East Germany's accession to West Germany on October 3rd, 1990 — only 329 days after the Wall fell. November 9th was rejected as a date because it also recalls the Reichspogromnacht of 1938. October 3rd has been a public holiday since 1990, replacing June 17th (the ‘Day of German Unity', commemorating the East German uprising of 1953).
german_unity_day.traditions: Each year a different German state hosts the central ‘Citizens' Festival' with an official ceremony. In Berlin a large open-air festival is held at the Brandenburg Gate with concerts, stages and citizen dialogues. Many states organize open-house events at ministries and constitutional bodies. The Chancellor traditionally gives a speech.
funFacts: In 2024 the central ‘Citizens' Festival' was held in Schwerin (Mecklenburg-Vorpommern) — every year the host city changes.
The original proposal of ‘Day of German Unity' on November 9th was rejected because of the date's historic baggage.
On October 3rd, 1990, a unified German flag flew officially in front of the Reichstag for the first time since 1949.
Legally, reunification only took one second — at midnight the Unification Treaty entered into force.
german_unity_day.imageCredit: Photo: א (Aleph) Creator: Johann Gottfried Schadow · CC BY-SA 2.5 via Wikimedia Commons

reformation_day.intro: Reformation Day on 31 October commemorates Martin Luther's launch of the Protestant Reformation in 1517.
reformation_day.history: On 31 October 1517 Martin Luther is said to have posted his Ninety-five Theses on the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg, challenging Church practices and sparking the Protestant Reformation. The movement reshaped Christianity and German society. It is a public holiday across much of Germany, especially the Protestant north and east.
reformation_day.traditions: Church services and concerts mark the day, particularly in Wittenberg and other Luther sites. In some regions children also celebrate with lanterns, and the 500th anniversary in 2017 drew large commemorations.
funFacts: Luther's Ninety-five Theses sparked the Reformation in 1517.
It is a public holiday in many German states.
Wittenberg's Castle Church is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
